author: @ColinWatters
text:
Great video. Its worth pointing out two things: 1) objects or rooms being heated will warm up until the power going into them is equal to the power being lost (eg through walls and windows).  2) All electrical resistive heaters (fan heaters, ceramic heaters etc) are 100% efficient. 

Together this means that the type of resistive heater you use has little impact on running costs if all else is the same. In short dont believe the hype some suppliers of electric heaters use. A cheap fan heater with a thermostat can be as good as an expensive electric radaitor. Oh and both are way more expensive than mains gas on a like for like basis. It would be a mistake for most people to rip out a gas central heating system and replace with electric radaitors. Heat pumps are a better bet but they have their own issues you need to understand if you want to save money. The priority should always be to spend on improving insulation before spending on new heating systems.

replies.author: @Guardian_Arias
replies.text: Heat pumps do come with their own set of cons but they can easily provide 2 watts of heating for each watt of electricity used and the standard is quickly becoming 3 to 1 watts. Sounds law breaking but thats because they use that single watt of energy to move heat from one place to an other instead of converting 1 watt of electrical potential into 1 watt of thermal energy. In addition, heat pumps have the added benefit of both heating and cooling a house, an absolute must for areas that see temps of negative 6c during the winter and 47c in the summer. Adding more insulation isn't necessarily more cost effective even over the long-term depending on specific use cases.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: very good points mate -thank you for taking the time to make them

replies.author: @kadmow
replies.text: @Guardian_Arias  - sadly the incentive schemes in  many areas don't allow a reverse cycle heat pump to qualify - disincentivising the cooling use-case in summer (when a small solar system easily provides grid independent daytime cooling - unloading the grid).

author: @ajempower
text: Is it possible to create an induction heated domestic boiler? And would it be a cost effective form of heating water in a domestic water heating system with radiators? I was thinking of a four induction heating hob or induction heaters below individual radiators and a circulation pump as they are usually steel and would respond to the induction process. May be a crazy idea?

replies.author: @JJFX-
replies.text:
Sure it's possible. They already exist, typically in the form of 'tankless' water heaters that heat only on demand. This can be more efficient but it's highly dependent on the situation. Problem is they require a significant amount of power which often means electrical panel upgrades to handle it. This gets more significant as necessary flow rate increases. There may also be long term considerations of the pipes being heated. 

For a boiler tank that uses induction, it's probably not practical or beneficial for typical uses. There are additional circuitry losses due to nature of induction compared to resistive heating. I don't see potential use cases for something like this focused on efficiency but that's not to say it isn't possible.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: tey already mak them mate - as to being more cost effective - i doubt it but i don't know

author: @justtinkering6713

replies.author: @justtinkering6713
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering If I close the vents in a room with forced air heat, aren't I controlling the heat in that room?

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I would say no - not really mate - i would say you were controlling how the heat generated is used - that is to heat the room or the outside. To control the heat you would really need to turn off heat generation when the room reached the temperature you wanted. But it's just what i think - and if you think differently - why not lol

replies.author: @justtinkering6713
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering So I think the title "Controlling heat generation" would be more accurate for your video. Thanks for all you do.

author: @someadvids5655
text: Hi, "im just asking" but, i was tought that Ohms law was V = I X "times" R not V + IR? because the way you sead it that the I and R was added together? why are you saying it differently? ok my training was in motor, magnets and transformer re-winding? but still im just asking? take care and have a great day!

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: it's common practice to say IR when I multiply R is meant - when I+R is meant then it is common practice to say I plus R the visuals also back up what was being said to make the operator disambiguous - you have a great day too mate
